label: Toyota Motor North America – Supply Chain & Procurement Divi

data.title: Toyota Motor North America – Supply Chain & Procurement Division: Organizational Decision-Flow Diagnostic Report
data.executive_summary: The most critical fault line in Toyota North America's supply chain organization is the structural misalignment between cost-reduction KPIs (owned by Finance and Procurement) and supply resilience investments (defended by Supply Chain leadership), which distorts decisions on semiconductor dual-sourcing, safety stock levels, and supplier development in ways that no single forum is currently equipped to resolve. A secondary but equally pervasive fault line runs between corporate Plano and plant-level operations, most sharply visible at Georgetown KY, where the Plant Supply Chain Manager (Marcus Thompson) routinely exercises de facto sourcing authority that bypasses formal procurement governance—a pattern tacitly endorsed by VP of Manufacturing James Mueller but never formalized. The S&OP process, nominally the organization's demand-supply balancing forum, is functionally compromised by a documented shadow forecasting practice among production planners who systematically distrust commercial inputs—a problem the S&OP Process Lead (Robert Park) has acknowledged but declined to formally escalate. Decision ownership is ambiguous or contested across at least seven high-stakes domains including semiconductor dual-sourcing rationalization, engineering change gate enforcement, supplier risk governance, and the logistics inclusion protocol for sourcing decisions, all of which lack a single named owner with both authority and accountability. The highest-priority diagnostic follow-up should focus on the governance gap between Michael Chen (VP Supply Chain) and the COO layer above him, where multiple unresolved cross-functional decisions—engineering change gates, resilience premium frameworks, the exception sourcing protocol—are stalled awaiting executive sponsorship that has not been formally requested.

sections.content:

**N01 (Michael Chen)** is the highest-degree node with 14 edges, serving as the structural hub connecting HQ, corporate procurement, logistics, planning, manufacturing, finance, and analytics. **N02 (Sarah Martinez)** and **N08 (Lisa Morrison)** are the next highest-degree nodes at 12 and 10 respectively, reflecting their cross-functional coordination roles. **N12 (Dr. Gregory Chen)** and **N05 (Marcus Thompson)** are the most structurally isolated nodes—Engineering has no direct meeting co-attendance edge with Finance or Logistics, and Georgetown has no direct edge to the Supplier Development or Analytics functions, representing meaningful visibility gaps.

sections.heading: Section 2: Decision Inventory
sections.content:
## Decision Inventory Table

| Decision Name | Primary Owner | Co-Owners / Influencers | Decision Type | Frequency | Meeting Venue | KPIs Affected | Ownership Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Enterprise supplier selection (direct materials) | Sarah Martinez, Dir. Procurement | Michael Chen (approval above threshold); Jennifer Holt (risk input); Patricia Lau (logistics input) | Strategic | Ad hoc / program cycle | Strategic Sourcing Review | PPV, Supply Diversification Ratio, Freight Cost % | Clear |
| ⚠ Semiconductor dual-sourcing rationalization | Contested: Michael Chen vs. Rajesh Patel | Sarah Martinez; David Okafor; HQ VP SC (informal veto) | Strategic | Annual (2025 plan) | No dedicated forum yet | Resilience Score, Inventory Carrying Cost, Procurement Cost Realization | Contested |
| ⚠ Safety stock level-setting (strategic components) | Michael Chen (claims ownership) | Rajesh Patel (finance pressure); Marcus Thompson (plant execution) | Operational | Quarterly / Annual plan | S&OP; QBR | Inventory Turns, Cash-to-Cash Cycle, On-time Delivery | Contested |
| Commodity contract renewal (semiconductors) | David Okafor | Sarah Martinez (strategic direction); Michael Chen (volume forecast input) | Operational | Ad hoc (contract cycle) | Commodity Review | Cost per Unit, Supply Risk Score, Expedited Order Cost | Clear |
| ⚠ Emergency / exception local sourcing (plant level) | Marcus Thompson (in practice) | James Mueller (tacit endorsement); Michael Chen (formal authority) | Operational | Ad hoc | No formal forum | OEE, On-time Inbound Delivery, Line Stoppages | Contested |
| Supplier watch-list designation | Jennifer Holt | Sarah Martinez (sourcing action approval); relevant commodity manager | Operational | Monthly trigger | Monthly Commodity Review | Supplier Quality Index, Supplier Financial Health Score | Clear |
| ⚠ Cross-functional supplier risk governance (standing forum) | Unassigned | Jennifer Holt (proposed); Michael Chen; Sarah Martinez; James Mueller | Strategic | Proposed (not standing) | No forum established | Supplier Financial Health, Line Stoppages, Dual-Source %, Resilience Score | Ambiguous |
| Production schedule (S&OP output) | Lisa Morrison | Robert Park (process); James Mueller (capacity); Michael Chen (supply constraints) | Operational | Monthly | S&OP Executive Session | Schedule Attainment, Production Plan Variance, OEE | Clear |
| ⚠ Demand forecast (commercial input) | Sarah Chen (commercial) | Robert Park (process); David Lim (shadow adjustment); Tom Bradley VP Sales (political override) | Operational | Monthly | S&OP Demand Review | Forecast Accuracy, Inventory Variance, Production Plan Variance | Contested |
| ⚠ Engineering change gate approval | Dr. Gregory Chen (engineering); David Park (program mgr.) | Lisa Morrison (supply chain impact); Sandra Park COO (escalation) | Strategic | Ad hoc | Program Management Reviews | Production Plan Variance, Expedite Cost, Schedule Attainment | Ambiguous |
| Inbound logistics network design | Patricia Lau | Sarah Martinez (sourcing decisions upstream); Michael Chen (strategic alignment) | Strategic | Annual / ad hoc | S&OP; Carrier QBRs | Freight Cost %, On-time Delivery, Expedite Rate | Clear |
| ⚠ Total landed cost framework (logistics inclusion in sourcing) | Unassigned | Patricia Lau (proposed); Sarah Martinez (resistant); Michael Chen (nominal owner) | Strategic | Should be systematic | No formal trigger | PPV, Freight Cost %, Procurement Cost Realization | Ambiguous |
| Analytics recommendation adoption | Function leads (Holt, Morrison, Lau) | Amara Okonkwo (recommends only); Michael Chen (air cover) | Operational | Monthly / ad hoc | Monthly Analytics Review; S&OP | Forecast Accuracy MAPE, Dashboard Adoption Rate | Ambiguous |
| Network optimization (DC repositioning) | Ambiguous: Michael Chen / Patricia Lau / Rajesh Patel | Amara Okonkwo (model owner) | Strategic | Active workstream | No dedicated forum established | Freight Cost %, Working Capital Days, Transit Cost per Unit | Ambiguous |

⚠ flags appear on 7 of 14 decisions, concentrated in the domains of resilience investment, engineering-supply chain interface, demand forecasting governance, and plant-level exception authority—the highest-risk decision domains in the organization.

sections.heading: Section 3: Meeting Map
sections.content:
## Meeting Inventory Table

| Meeting Name | Frequency | Confirmed Attendees | Decision Outputs | Classification | Issues Flagged |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Executive S&OP | Monthly | Michael Chen (chair), Robert Park, Lisa Morrison, James Mueller, Patricia Lau, Amara Okonkwo (embedded), Sarah Chen (commercial), Tom Bradley (VP Sales) | Production plan approval, inventory position sign-off, escalated forecast disputes | Mixed | Overdense; forecast accountability avoided by chair |
| S&OP Demand Review | Monthly (pre-Executive S&OP) | Robert Park (facilitator), David Lim (planning mgr.), Sarah Chen (commercial forecast owner) | Demand forecast input to production plan | Informational | Underdense; shadow forecast adjustments happen outside this forum |
| Monthly Commodity Review | Monthly | Sarah Martinez (chair), David Okafor, Jennifer Holt (when quality/risk issue present), relevant senior buyers | Commodity strategy updates, watch-list flags, sourcing action triggers | Mixed | None flagged; medium-priority risk items lack follow-through |
| Biweekly Production-Logistics Touchpoint | Biweekly | Lisa Morrison, Patricia Lau (4-5 each side) | Expedite requests, revised delivery windows, supplier constraint escalations to procurement | Decision-Producing | None |
| Plant Operations Weekly Meeting | Weekly | Marcus Thompson, Plant Manager Georgetown, manufacturing leadership | Supply-production alignment for coming week, supplier escalation flags | Decision-Producing | Plant-only; no Plano participation |
| Daily Stand-Up (Georgetown) | Daily | Marcus Thompson, inbound logistics supervisors, line-side supervisors | Tactical: expedite calls, safety stock draws, delivery exception handling | Decision-Producing | None |
| Executive Operations Council | Monthly | Sandra Park (COO, chair), James Mueller, Michael Chen, Lisa Morrison, peers | Resource allocation, cross-functional priority alignment, escalated disputes | Mixed | Underdense on supply chain-engineering tensions; no formal decision log cited |
| Plant Manager Quarterly Meeting | Quarterly | James Mueller (chair), all NA plant managers including Georgetown | Operational performance across plants, enterprise standards, best practice sharing | Informational | Underdense on supply chain exception protocol |
| Monthly Analytics Review | Monthly | Amara Okonkwo (chair), supply chain function leads (variable attendance) | Analytics KPI updates, model performance — awareness only | Performative | Underdense; no decisions produced |
| Carrier Quarterly Business Reviews | Quarterly | Patricia Lau (owner), carrier partners | Carrier scorecards, contract adjustments, performance improvement plans | Decision-Producing | None |
| Program Management Reviews | Monthly | Dr. Gregory Chen, program managers (incl. David Park), CPO | Gate approvals/holds, late change log review, schedule attainment | Decision-Producing | Engineering-supply chain notification gap not addressed |
| Design-for-Manufacturability Sessions | Quarterly | Dr. Gregory Chen, Rachel Huang (procurement rep), manufacturing rep | Design simplification, tooling decisions — quality variable | Mixed | Underdense on supply chain strategic participation; attendance inconsistent |
| Supply Resilience Working Group | Defunct (was biweekly 2021-22) | Michael Chen (chair), David Okafor, logistics, manufacturing, finance (observer) | Supplier risk profiles, scenario modeling — now inactive | Defunct | Was decision-producing; lost momentum post-shortage normalization |

The **Executive S&OP** and **Monthly Commodity Review** carry the highest decision-producing density but are both flagged for structural issues: the Executive S&OP is overdense with competing agenda items that crowd out accountability conversations on forecast accuracy, while the Commodity Review lacks a formal mechanism to escalate medium-priority supplier risks into sourcing actions. The **Monthly Analytics Review** and **Plant Manager Quarterly Meeting** are the clearest examples of performative or underdense forums—both run on regular cadence but produce no documented decisions and do not connect to the formal decision flow in any traceable way.

sections.heading: Section 4: KPI Conflict Analysis
sections.content:
## KPI Conflict Pairs

| KPI A (Owner, Metric) | KPI B (Owner, Metric) | Nature of Conflict | Decisions Distorted | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rajesh Patel: Inventory Carrying Cost <18% of procurement spend | Michael Chen: Supply Chain Resilience Score (dual-source %, safety stock coverage) | Reducing safety stock and consolidating to single-source improves working capital but degrades resilience; optimizing resilience inflates carrying cost | Semiconductor dual-source rationalization; safety stock level-setting; 2025 operating plan | High |
| Sarah Martinez: PPV reduction 3% YoY | Patricia Lau: Freight Cost <5% of procurement spend | Procurement's unit cost optimization (supplier consolidation, offshore sourcing) increases freight complexity and expedite premiums, degrading Lau's metrics without appearing in Martinez's scorecard | Supplier location decisions; logistics inclusion in sourcing; semiconductor dual-source | High |
| Rajesh Patel: Procurement Cost Realization 90% | David Okafor: Supply Risk Score (directional, composite) | Dual-sourcing premium compresses realized savings; risk score justification carries no hard dollar target and loses in budget conversations against a numeric realization shortfall | Dual-source contract flexibility; volume exclusivity trade-offs | High |
| James Mueller: OEE ≥95% / Zero Line Stoppages | Michael Chen: On-time Delivery to Plants ≥98% / Total Landed Cost –2% | Mueller's OEE mandate creates pressure for exception local sourcing that violates enterprise supplier agreements, undermining Chen's cost and compliance targets | Emergency exception sourcing (Georgetown); supplier agreement compliance | Medium |
| Robert Park: Forecast Accuracy ±10% at model level | Tom Bradley (Sales VP): No formal forecast accuracy KPI on commercial side | Supply chain carries accountability for a metric substantially driven by commercial inputs that commercial leadership is not accountable for | Demand review credibility; shadow forecasting; production plan variance | High |
| Lisa Morrison: Production Plan Variance <3% | Dr. Gregory Chen: Program Schedule Attainment 90% (gate-based) | Engineering's gate attainment metric does not penalize late changes that occur *after* design freeze; changes that slip post-freeze create plan variance for Morrison but register as on-time for Chen's scorecard | Engineering change gate enforcement; production plan variance | High |
| Amara Okonkwo: Forecast MAPE improvement –15pp vs. commercial forecast | Robert Park: S&OP Process Compliance 95% | Okonkwo's model outperforms the commercial forecast (her KPI) but planners informally use shadow adjustments rather than formally adopting her model output, creating a compliance gap Robert cannot surface without exposing the shadow forecasting practice | Demand sensing adoption; S&OP process integrity | Medium |

**The most systemic KPI conflict pattern** is the structural separation of cost-reduction accountability from resilience-investment accountability across the procurement-finance axis. Sarah Martinez's 3% PPV target and Rajesh Patel's 90% cost realization target both create direct pressure to consolidate suppliers and reduce dual-sourcing premiums, while Michael Chen's resilience score and David Okafor's supply risk composite carry no equivalent financial weight in budget or performance review processes. This asymmetry—hard cost KPIs on one side, soft directional risk indicators on the other—means that in any resource or governance conversation, cost arguments win by default. The same asymmetry appears in the manufacturing-engineering interface: OEE and production plan variance are hard tracked, but the engineering change contribution to those variances is not captured in any KPI that engineering leadership is accountable for.

sections.heading: Section 5: Fault Line Analysis
sections.content:
**Fault Line 1: Plant Operations vs. Corporate Procurement**
- **Evidence:** Marcus Thompson (Georgetown): *"What I own outright... expedited or emergency local sourcing when the schedule is at risk. That last one is technically in a gray zone formally, but operationally I own it because I'm the one who has to keep the plant running."* James Mueller tacitly endorsed the Georgetown exception sourcing decision by framing it as a manufacturing standpoint judgment, not a process violation. Sarah Martinez described losing an enterprise fastener agreement enforcement against Princeton plant, resulting in ~$400K annual pricing tier shortfall.
- **Decision Domains Affected:** Emergency exception sourcing; enterprise supplier agreement compliance; plant-level safety stock levels; supplier qualification bypass.
- **Prevalence:** Surfaced in 6 of 12 interviews (Thompson, Mueller, Chen M., Martinez, Lau, Morrison).

**Fault Line 2: Finance vs. Supply Chain on Resilience Investment**
- **Evidence:** Rajesh Patel: *"The CFO's chief of staff pushed back... if lead times had normalized, the risk justification for the slow drawdown wasn't there."* David Okafor noted that his supply risk score *"doesn't carry the same weight as a cost number"* in budget conversations. Michael Chen described dual-sourcing expansion being *"significantly shaped by anticipated HQ reaction"* before formal proposal.
- **Decision Domains Affected:** Semiconductor dual-sourcing rationalization; safety stock level-setting; 2025 operating plan working capital targets; supplier resilience working group resourcing.
- **Prevalence:** Surfaced in 7 of 12 interviews (Chen M., Martinez, Okafor, Patel, Mueller, Lau, Holt).

**Fault Line 3: HQ Japan vs. North America Decision Rights**
- **Evidence:** Michael Chen described decisions being *"significantly shaped by anticipated HQ reaction before they ever became formal proposals"* and the dual-sourcing expansion being walked back after a bilateral call with the HQ VP of Supply Chain. Chen acknowledged: *"Nobody puts a block in writing if they can avoid it — you have a conversation, the message lands, and the decision adjusts."* David Okafor noted the supply resilience working group lost momentum partly because *"the narrative shifted"* toward cost normalization aligned with HQ priorities.
- **Decision Domains Affected:** Dual-sourcing investment scale; strategic safety stock philosophy; supplier qualification thresholds; quarterly business review framing.
- **Prevalence:** Surfaced in 4 of 12 interviews (Chen M., Okafor, Martinez, Patel).

**Fault Line 4: Engineering vs. Supply Chain on ECN Lead Time**
- **Evidence:** Lisa Morrison: *"There's no formal gate process to prevent it from happening again. I can flag it, I can escalate to Michael, but I don't have an enforcement mechanism."* Dr. Gregory Chen acknowledged the thermal management ECN created *"real conflict"* with Lisa's team but defended engineering's need for *"flexibility to respond to quality or regulatory issues."* Jennifer Holt quantified expedited freight costs from ECN-driven retrofits.
- **Decision Domains Affected:** Engineering change gate enforcement; production plan variance accountability; supplier lead time commitments; design-for-manufacturability session authority.
- **Prevalence:** Surfaced in 5 of 12 interviews (Morrison, Chen G., Holt, Mueller, Patel).

**Fault Line 5: TPS-Trained Cohort vs. Digital/Analytics Cohort**
- **Evidence:** Amara Okonkwo described planners rejecting her demand sensing model with the phrase *"the model doesn't know what we know about those customer conversations."* Robert Park acknowledged shadow forecasting by experienced senior planners as *"people with institutional knowledge applying what they genuinely believe is better judgment."* Okonkwo noted Michael Chen *"engages most readily when I bring him a concrete operational problem"* rather than abstract model recommendations.
- **Decision Domains Affected:** Demand forecast adoption; analytics recommendation implementation; S&OP agenda structure; supplier risk model utilization.
- **Prevalence:** Surfaced in 4 of 12 interviews (Okonkwo, Park R., Morrison, Chen M.).

**Fault Line 6: Procurement vs. Logistics on Total Landed Cost**
- **Evidence:** Patricia Lau: *"Procurement's metrics are built around unit cost and savings against benchmark. Transportation cost doesn't show up in their scorecard in a way that creates urgency to involve me earlier."* Lau was *"brought in after the sourcing decision was essentially made"* on semiconductor dual-sourcing. The cross-functional sourcing review process is *"inconsistently applied"* when procurement is moving quickly.
- **Decision Domains Affected:** Logistics inclusion in sourcing decisions; semiconductor dual-source network cost; total landed cost framework ownership.
- **Prevalence:** Surfaced in 5 of 12 interviews (Lau, Martinez, Okafor, Patel, Chen M.).

sections.heading: Section 6: Opportunity Zones
sections.content:
1. **S&OP Forecast Accountability Gap**
The Executive S&OP meeting has no formal mechanism to assign ownership of forecast accuracy corrections: a systematic commercial over-forecast has been documented for at least three consecutive months on specific model lines, the root cause has been identified in meeting post-mortems, yet no action owner has been captured in the decision log.
*Diagnostic question: What would need to change in the S&OP governance structure—agenda design, decision documentation, or attendee accountability—for the forecast accuracy gap to produce a named corrective action rather than a bilaterally managed side conversation?*

2. **Shadow Forecast Legitimacy Question**
Senior planners are systematically adjusting commercial forecasts downward before using them in production decisions, and the S&OP Process Lead has knowingly refrained from formally surfacing this practice for approximately 18 months.
*Diagnostic question: Is the shadow forecast a symptom of a broken commercial forecasting process that should be formalized and governed, or is it an informal quality control mechanism that the organization depends on but cannot officially sanction?*

3. **Resilience Investment Framework Absence**
The 2025 operating plan requires a resolved position on semiconductor dual-sourcing premiums by late October, but the analytical framework to make that decision is not yet resourced or in progress, and the cross-functional team needed to build it (Supply Chain risk + Procurement + Finance) has not formally mobilized.
*Diagnostic question: Is the absence of a shared risk-adjusted framework for evaluating resilience investments a resource problem, a governance problem, or a reflection of genuine disagreement about whether such a framework can produce a number that all parties will accept?*

4. **Engineering Change Gate Authority Vacuum**
A formal engineering change gate requiring supply chain approval has been discussed between Lisa Morrison and Dr. Gregory Chen for over a year, has COO-level visibility in operational reviews, but has not been formally proposed as a structured governance change with cost data and a named proposal owner.
*Diagnostic question: What is the minimum governance structure—gate criteria, approval authority, emergency exception path—that would satisfy both engineering's flexibility needs and supply chain's planning stability requirements, and who has the cross-functional standing to propose it?*

5. **Plant Exception Sourcing Protocol**
The Georgetown sourcing exception has established a de facto precedent for plant-level bypass of enterprise supplier agreements, tacitly endorsed by two VPs but not formalized into a tiered exception protocol that other plants could invoke consistently.
*Diagnostic question: Does the organization intend to formalize a tiered exception sourcing protocol, and if so, who owns the design of that protocol and what threshold criteria would distinguish a legitimate plant emergency from a preference-driven bypass?*

6. **Analytics Structural Positioning**
Amara Okonkwo's demand sensing model demonstrably outperforms the commercial forecast on MAPE, but her analysis is embedded as a supplement within planning's S&OP section rather than presented as an independent input—a structural positioning that subordinates the output before it reaches decision-makers.
*Diagnostic question: Does the current meeting structure and reporting relationship for the analytics function match the level of organizational authority needed to drive adoption of recommendations that challenge incumbent judgment-based practices?*

7. **Logistics-Procurement Coordination Trigger**
A total landed cost framework that includes logistics input in sourcing decisions above a spend threshold exists in policy but is inconsistently applied; Patricia Lau has raised this repeatedly in S&OP without producing a process commitment, and the incentive structure (procurement KPIs exclude freight cost) does not reinforce compliance.
*Diagnostic question: Is the total landed cost framework a process design problem solvable through clearer trigger criteria, or does it require a KPI restructuring on the procurement side before behavioral change is achievable?*

8. **Supplier Risk Governance Orphan**
The supply resilience working group formed in 2021 produced substantive risk artifacts (single-point-of-failure maps, Taiwan Strait scenario models) but disbanded as shortage urgency faded; no standing forum now owns proactive supplier risk governance at a cross-functional level.
*Diagnostic question: Is there a current organizational owner willing and positioned to sponsor a standing supplier risk governance forum with cross-functional decision rights, and what would be required to distinguish it structurally from the commodity reviews and S&OP meetings that currently absorb but do not resolve these risks?*

sections.heading: Section 7: Individual Interview Profiles (Appendix)
sections.content:
---
### Profile 1: Michael Chen — VP of Supply Chain, North America
**Reports To:** COO, Toyota Motor North America (Sandra Park implied; direct line); dotted-line to HQ VP of Supply Chain (Japan)
**Direct Reports:** Sarah Martinez (Dir. Procurement), Jennifer Holt (Dir. Supplier Development), Patricia Lau (Dir. Logistics – Inbound), Amara Okonkwo (Sr. Mgr. Analytics)

**KPIs:**
- Total landed cost reduction: 2% YoY
- Supply chain resilience score: composite (dual-source %, supplier financial health, geographic diversity); directional vs. 2022 baseline
- On-time delivery to plants: ≥98%
- Inventory turns on strategic safety stock: under active tension with Finance

**Key Meetings:**
- Executive S&OP (monthly, chair): approves production plan, escalates forecast disputes
- Executive Operations Council (monthly, participant): cross-functional priority alignment
- Quarterly Business Review with HQ VP SC (quarterly, presenter): 5-KPI package, major sourcing decisions, risk flags
- Monthly HQ bilateral check-in (monthly, presenter): standing update, informal signal detection

**Decisions Owned:**
- Strategic: dual-sourcing investment scale (co-owned with HQ, above threshold)
- Strategic: supply chain resilience philosophy and safety stock philosophy
- Operational: Tier 2 supplier financial intervention authorization
- Operational: enterprise exception sourcing endorsement (tacit)

**Key Interfaces:**
- HQ VP SC (Japan): structured reporting + informal signal channel
- Rajesh Patel (Finance): contested resilience premium debate
- James Mueller (Manufacturing VP): exception sourcing alignment; tiered protocol discussion
- COO: briefed proactively; formal escalation rarely used

**Pain Points:**
- *"I've had decisions significantly shaped by anticipated HQ reaction before they ever became formal proposals."*
- Dual-sourcing expansion walked back informally via bilateral HQ call before formal proposal.

**Workarounds:**
- Initiates bilateral calls with HQ VP before formal proposals to test reaction and pre-shape decisions.
- Briefs COO proactively on decisions near authority boundary to avoid post-hoc accountability.

**Node-Edge Summary:**
- N01 → [Reporting Line] COO (Sandra Park)
- N01 → [Reporting Line dotted] HQ VP Supply Chain (Japan)
- N01 ← [Reporting Line] N02, N04, N06, N11
- N01 ↔ [Meeting: Executive S&OP] N07, N08, N09, N06, N11
- N01 ↔ [Meeting: Executive Operations Council] N09, N08
- N01 ↔ [KPI Conflict: Resilience vs. Working Capital] N10
- N01 ↔ [Decision Interdependency: Exception Sourcing] N09, N05
- N01 ↔ [Decision Interdependency: Dual-Source Rationalization] N02, N03, N10

---
### Profile 2: Sarah Martinez — Director of Procurement, Direct Materials
**Reports To:** Michael Chen (VP Supply Chain)
**Direct Reports:** David Okafor (Commodity Mgr. Semiconductors); additional commodity managers (named generically)

**KPIs:**
- PPV: 3% YoY reduction on direct materials (hard target)
- Supplier cost competitiveness: at or below market median quarterly
- Supply diversification ratio: commodity-specific floors (semiconductors ≥2 sources for >$5M spend; steel ≥3 sources per grade)
- Supplier on-time delivery: part of shared scorecard
- Contract compliance rate: directional

**Key Meetings:**
- Monthly Commodity Review (chair): watch-list flags, sourcing action triggers, strategy updates
- Strategic procurement planning meetings (periodic, chair): sourcing direction setting
- S&OP (participant): supply-side inputs

**Decisions Owned:**
- Strategic: enterprise supplier selection and contract structure (sole authority within threshold)
- Operational: volume split decisions (e.g., 30/70 incumbent/alternate split on steel panels)
- Transactional: supplier escalation approvals from Jennifer Holt's watch-list flags

**Key Interfaces:**
- Jennifer Holt: receives risk signals; approves or delays sourcing actions
- Patricia Lau: logistics cost data input (received post-decision, not pre-decision)
- Marcus Thompson / Georgetown: plant acceptance of new suppliers (source of friction)
- Michael Chen: escalation for plant non-compliance; alignment on dual-source philosophy

**Pain Points:**
- *"What [Michael] didn't do was explicitly direct Georgetown to accept the supplier split. He created the conditions."* — indirect escalation support limits enforcement.
- Princeton plant non-compliance with fastener enterprise agreement: ~$400K annual pricing tier shortfall unresolved.

**Workarounds:**
- Uses Michael Chen's brief attendance in plant meetings to signal organizational priority without him directing outcomes.
- Tracks procurement-owned risk register monthly but acknowledges it is not a corporate governance document.

**Node-Edge Summary:**
- N02 → [Reporting Line] N01
- N02 ← [Reporting Line] N03
- N02 ↔ [Meeting: Monthly Commodity Review] N04, N03
- N02 ↔ [Meeting: S&OP] N07
- N02 ↔ [KPI Conflict: PPV vs. Freight Cost] N06
- N02 ↔ [Decision Interdependency: Supplier Selection] N04, N05, N06
- N02 ↔ [KPI Conflict: PPV vs. Dual-Source Premium] N10

---
### Profile 3: David Okafor — Commodity Manager, Semiconductors & Electronics
**Reports To:** Sarah Martinez (Dir. Procurement)
**Direct Reports:** None stated

**KPIs:**
- Cost per unit: 2% YoY reduction (hard target, primary friction source)
- Supplier on-time delivery: 98%
- Supply risk score: composite (financial health, geopolitical exposure, capacity utilization) — directional, no hard target
- Expedited order cost: informal "keep low" directive; no hard budget line

**Key Meetings:**
